conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

First, do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I have a son, Alex. He's 21 now, but I wanted to check if I might still be eligible for Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to understand his current situation.

Is Alex currently in full-time approved education or training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any education or training at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to fully assess your eligibility.

Is Alex currently registered with a career service or with Connexions (or equivalent service), and has he left education or training within the last 20 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with any career service, and it's been quite a while since he left education.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to ask one more important question to complete my assessment.

Are you responsible for any other children under the age of 20?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex is my only child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

**For Alex:** He is 21 years old and is not currently in full-time approved education or training. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can generally claim Child Benefit for children under 16, or under 20 if they are in approved full-time education or training. Since Alex is 21 and not in education or training, he does not meet the age and education criteria required for Child Benefit eligibility.

Child Benefit typically stops when a young person turns 20, or earlier if they leave full-time education or training before that age. I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 21 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 21, which is 20 or over
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£20.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 21 years old and is not currently in full-time approved education or training. Under UK Child Benefit rules, you can generally claim Child Benefit for children under 16, or under 20 if they are in approved full-time education or training. Since Alex is 21 and not in education or training, he does not meet the age and education criteria required for Child Benefit eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 21 years old and not in any education or training. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or under 20 if they remain in approved full-time education or training. Since Alex exceeds the maximum age threshold and is not in qualifying education, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.

Since Alex exceeds the maximum age threshold and is not in qualifying education, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for him.
